title: Chapter 25: A Stranger Among His Own
paragraphs: Agdash never slept. The city of the fire elves lived in its own eternal, feverish rhythm — somewhere above, on the aristocratic terraces, dim magical lanterns flickered; below, in the industrial tiers, the heavy percussion of hammers never ceased. The smell of smoked meat and scorching oil seeped from narrow alleyways. The world beyond the walls continued to rot, but inside these black basalt walls, people clung to a semblance of life with the desperation of the drowning.
Anar walked through the lower tier alone. Without purpose, without direction, without meaning.

He had been walking for three hours and in that time had completed a full loop of the merchant quarter, crossed the Smiths' Bridge twice, and passed the same murky dive bar three times, from which came strained, drunken laughter. His hands were shoved deep into the pockets of trousers re-stitched in the local fashion, his shoulders hunched, and on his face was frozen the expression his friends called 'default indifference.' But today, beneath that mask, there was no indifference. Beneath it was emptiness — real, cold, weightless emptiness that made you want to howl.

Zhenya had been holed up in the Mechanics' Guild since dawn. Anar had stopped by that morning — his friend was elbow-deep in machine oil, frantically soldering something with the one-eyed dwarf Brann, and when Anar called out to him, Zhenya merely waved his hand absently: 'Bro, hang on, I've got a valve calibration at critical, we'll chat later.' Later never came. Anar stood by the workbench for twenty minutes, watching Zhenya obsessively fiddle with gears, and quietly left when it became clear his presence went unnoticed.

Yura was at the mine. Foreman of the Eastern Slope. His voice, amplified by a tin megaphone, carried across the quarry like a tocsin. Elf miners who just two weeks ago had contemptuously muttered 'human runt' now followed his instructions with military discipline. Yura had found his element in chaos — and chaos had submitted to him.

Ravan was not only training on the lower platforms, pulverizing basalt practice pillars with his pneumatic fist, but he also didn't miss a single opportunity to roast Anar. He joked constantly, sarcastically mocking his laziness and indifference, trying in this rough, masculine way to toughen his friend's character and make him at least a little angry at himself. Ravan knew exactly why he was here: to one day punch a path home.

Alexander had gone to the palace. Leader, diplomat, negotiator — Sasha was conducting some endless, hours-long meetings with King Agsham's advisors. He was building bridges between the earthly humans and the agonizing elven society. His mind, tempered by logic and strategy, was needed here like air.
Everyone was busy with their own tasks that moved them all forward.

Anar stopped at the edge of a wide street leading to the Trade Gates. He leaned against the rough basalt wall, feeling its cold through his jacket. What was the use of being in the safest place of a dead world if inside you remained absolute emptiness?
Anar shoved his hands back into his pockets and pushed off the wall with a heavy sigh.

At the Trade Gates, a wide passage between the lower tier and the Garrison Square, two guards stood watch. Fire elves in polished, if worn, red-and-gold breastplates. On both their heads, the characteristic orange flame flickered dimly, almost sluggishly — in most guards it had long lost its brightness, drained by endless shifts and chronic sleep deprivation.

One of them, tall and sharp-cheeked with unpleasantly thin lips and an arrogantly squinting gaze, was chewing some dry root and spitting brown saliva directly onto the cobblestones. The second, younger, nervously fidgeted with the shaft of his halberd.
Anar approached them.

He didn't really know why he was approaching. Maybe he wanted to ask if there was work. Maybe he wanted to know where to go. Or maybe he just wanted someone to look at him and see — not an empty space, but a living person.
'Hey, man,' Anar began quietly, addressing the sharp-cheeked one. 'Can you tell me where I could—'
The guard didn't let him finish. Without even turning his head, he drawled with an indifferent, chronic, bone-deep contempt:
'Move along, beggar. Don't interfere with the watch.'
Anar blinked. Something clicked inside — quietly but palpably. Like a rusty fuse that had always held suddenly developing a crack.
'I'm not a beggar,' Anar replied evenly. 'I actually travel with Avotu. I wouldn't talk to me like that if I were you.'

The guard finally stopped chewing. He slowly turned his head and swept Anar with a keen, assessing look — dirty trousers re-stitched in the local style, an earthly jacket mottled with soot, a thin, sharp-boned face with circles under the eyes. No armor, no weapons, not a single mark of belonging to the city guard.
'Or what?' the guard drawled with a lazy, provocative smirk, drawing up to his full, considerable height.

'What do you mean, "or what"?' Anar felt blood rushing to his temples. 'What's wrong with you? Are you out of your mind talking to people like that? I came to you with a normal question and you're out here acting tough.'
The guard stopped smiling. His eyes went cold, like cooled slag. He took a step forward, looming over the human.

'Listen here, outlander. There are hundreds like you in our city. Ragged drifters from the wastelands. Nobodies. And every other one drops someone's name. You can invoke King Agsham himself for all I care — I don't give a damn. You're nobody. And I'm the watchman of the Trade Gates. And you're annoying me.'

Anar clenched his teeth. Everything suffocating, everything accumulated during days of aimless wandering through the city, swelled inside him. The feeling of uselessness, purposelessness, meaninglessness. And now this one — standing there, baring his teeth, looking down, spitting brown chew.
'Where I'm from, we call people like you trash,' Anar said quietly, through clenched teeth, and in his voice rang an ominous, predatory metal.
The sharp-cheeked guard turned crimson. The flame on his head jerked, flaring brighter. He spun sharply to the younger one:
'Tarik! Help me tie up this insolent little human. Let him sit in the dungeon and think about how one should address the watch.'

Anar stepped back. A familiar electrical impulse shot down his spine like lightning, his fingers instinctively clenched, ready to discharge. But something stopped him. Perhaps the understanding that striking a guard in your own city meant putting not just yourself but all your people at risk. Perhaps — the remnants of common sense still clinging to the walls of his inflamed consciousness.
'What the hell is wrong with you—' Anar managed to spit out before the young guard, with a practiced, expert move, twisted his arm behind his back.

The ropes bit painfully into his wrists. Anar jerked, but the second guard was already pressing down from above, pinning him with a knee. The sharp-cheeked one leaned into his face, hitting him with the sour smell of chewing root:
'Fifteen days for an administrative violation and public insult of the watch. You'll have plenty of time to think about manners.'

The cell in Agdash's city dungeon was carved directly into volcanic rock. Four walls of raw basalt, coated in slippery, cold moisture. A dim reddish light emanated from a single crystal embedded in the ceiling — it gave just enough glow to make out the outlines of objects, but not enough to keep you from stumbling.

The smell hit Anar in the face like a sledgehammer. A sour, heavy, multilayered stench in which rancid sweat, spoiled food, urine, and something else — chemically caustic, something that made your eyes water — all blended together.

On the upper bunk — rough wooden planks nailed to the wall with rusty brackets — lay an enormous greenish mass. It was an orc. Massive, broad-shouldered, with a meaty face covered in coarse ginger stubble. His small eyes were cloudy and red, like those of a person who hadn't slept — or stopped drinking — in a very, very long time.
Anar crossed the threshold. The heavy grate clanged shut behind him.
'Good evening,' Anar said automatically, out of earthly habit, surveying the cell with the expression of a man examining his own nightmare.
The orc propped himself up on an elbow. His broad face spread into an unexpectedly good-natured, if thoroughly drunk, grin.
'Evening, neighbor,' he rasped in a voice like a rusty saw on wet wood. 'I cleared the bottom bunk for you. Make yourself at home.'

Anar looked at the bottom bunk. On it lay a crumpled rag of indeterminate color, covered with suspicious stains. The floor beneath the planks was strewn with bones of small animals, scraps of fabric, nut shells, and something else whose nature Anar preferred not to contemplate. The mess wasn't merely mess — it was an archaeology of neglect, a cultural layer of someone's catastrophically unkempt existence.
'Hey! Guards!' Anar spun toward the grate, gripping the rusty bars. 'I need to contact my friends! I have people who can—'

'Shut up,' came the indifferent voice from around the corridor corner. 'When you've served your fifteen days for the administrative violation — then you'll get out. And contact your friends. Until then — don't yell.'

Anar slowly unclenched his fingers from the bars. He stood, staring down the empty, dimly lit corridor, and felt the reality of his situation settle on his shoulders slowly, with the weight of a concrete slab. Fifteen days. Fifteen days in this stinking hole with a drunk orc. While his friends above were building, forging, commanding — finding their meaning in this rotting world — he would rot here, because he couldn't hold his tongue.
Anar sat heavily on the bottom bunk. The wood groaned pitifully. He buried his face in his palms and sat that way for a long, unbearably long time, listening to the gurgling snoring of the orc above.
The first night became the longest of his life.

The orc — his name was Gryz — didn't sleep. He drank. Through some supernatural, inexplicable means, he possessed an inexhaustible supply of murky liquid in a clay flask that smelled simultaneously of moonshine, rotten cabbage, and liquid misery. Gryz drank, then belched loudly and viscerally, then muttered something incoherent about some grandfather who once forged swords in some gorge, then drank again.
And he farted.

He did this with terrifying regularity — roughly every ten minutes — and with such acoustic power that the cell walls seemed to vibrate faintly. The sound was heavy, wet, rolling, and after each volley the cell filled not just with stench but with something dense, almost tangible, so thick that Anar was ready to swear it could have been used to chop wood.
'Hey, guards!' Anar jumped to the grate again, pressing his sleeve to his face. 'Are you giving him booze or what?! Drinking alcohol in a cell is supposed to be forbidden!'
'It's not forbidden!' came the same indifferent tone from around the corner.
Gryz, hearing the exchange, hung his heavy, meaty head over the edge of the upper bunk. His cloudy little eyes narrowed.
'What, trying to set me up or something?' the orc grunted, offended.

'Listen, you're killing me,' Anar turned to face him. Exhaustion and irritation had burned away the last traces of diplomacy. 'Why do you keep drinking? You stink beyond belief. When did you even last bathe?'
For a second, silence hung in the cell. Even the crystal in the ceiling seemed to flicker a shade dimmer.
'Right then,' Gryz said calmly, almost tenderly. 'You've said enough.'

The blow came from above. Enormous, heavy, like a collapsing ceiling. The orc simply leaned over the edge of the bunk and slammed his fist into Anar's cheekbone with full force. A greenish fist encrusted with grime, the size of a child's head.

For a fraction of a second the world became a white flash, then a kaleidoscope of red and black spots. Anar flew into the wall and crashed to the stone floor, the back of his head hitting stone. His mouth instantly filled with a salty, coppery taste of blood. Drops began to fall from his lip onto the dirty stone.
The pain was stunning, animal, primal. But stronger than the pain was the humiliation. Anar lay on the floor of a stinking cell, beaten by a drunk orc.
Guards came running at the noise. Two watchmen peered through the bars, indifferently assessed the situation.
'Listen, guards!' Anar, groaning, rose to all fours, spitting blood. 'I won't let this slide. When I get out — I'll tell Avotu everything. He'll make problems for you!'
The guards exchanged glances. The sharp-cheeked one — that very same one from the Trade Gates — was among those on duty. He leaned toward the grate with an unpleasant, deliberately sympathetic smile:
'Avotu has no influence here. Our city has its own laws and its own order. I'd advise you to calm down and serve your time quietly.'
'We'll see about that,' Anar croaked, wiping blood from his split lip with the back of his hand.
Fifteen days.

The first three days Anar didn't sleep at all. He lay on the bottom bunk, staring at the dark wood of the upper planks, feeling with every cell of his body the slow, suffocating flow of time. Gryz, as it turned out, wasn't such a monster after all. Sobering up on the second night, the orc even attempted an awkward bit of small talk, but Anar answered in monosyllables, and the conversation quickly died.
On the fourth day, Anar discovered he had gotten used to the smell. This discovery horrified him more than the smell itself.

Days were identical, like scabs on a wound. In the morning they brought a bowl of gray, sticky porridge, similar in consistency to putty and in taste to squeezed cardboard. In the evening — the same porridge. Between meals there was nothing but walls, silence, and thoughts heavy as tombstones.
Anar thought. About this world — hopeless, rotting, having devoured their familiar reality. About his hometown, about the warm radiators in the apartment. And about himself — lazy and aimless.

On the seventh day, Gryz, having once again mystically acquired liquor, woke Anar at three in the morning with a wild, deranged song in the orcish language. The song was about a dead wife, about some field beyond a river, and about how the sky used to be blue. Anar listened without interrupting. For the first time he saw in the orc's cloudy little eyes not stupidity, but the same familiar, painfully recognizable — emptiness. Gryz, too, was a stranger among his own.

On the tenth day, out of sheer boredom, Anar began training his mana control. He lay on his back and slowly, carefully, shuttled a tiny blue spark from one finger to another. Forward. Back. In a circle. He remembered Avotu's lessons: 'Mana is not a muscle. It's a river. You don't pull it — you guide it.' The spark obediently flowed between his knuckles, even and quiet, like the pulse of a sleeping child.
Gryz, watching from the upper bunk, whistled:
'So you're a mage, little human?'
'No. I'm just an idiot sitting in prison instead of doing something useful.'
On the fifteenth morning, the grate clanged open. The duty guard — this time an unfamiliar one, young, with a tired but not unkind face — nodded toward the exit:
'You're free. Don't get caught again.'

Anar stood. His muscles ached from inactivity, his head spun from meager nutrition, and his clothes had absorbed such an inconceivable amount of foreign odors that they seemed capable of standing upright on their own, separate from their owner.
Gryz, hanging over the edge of the upper bunk, extended an enormous, dirty palm:
'Take care, neighbor. You're all right. For a human.'
Anar shook his hand. Not because he wanted to, but because after fifteen days in one cell with a creature embodying everything an orc could possibly embody, refusing a handshake seemed somehow petty.

The evening Agdash greeted him with the dim flicker of magical lanterns and the dense smell of fried fish from the river canals. The sky overhead, as always, was leaden, heavy, dead. But after fifteen days of a ceiling half a meter above his face, even this hopeless sky seemed unbearably beautiful.
'Forget it,' Anar said quietly to himself, breathing relatively fresh air. 'I'll go to the tavern. Sleep there tonight. Figure things out tomorrow.'

The tavern called 'The Red Cauldron' sat at the junction of the merchant and residential tiers. Anar pushed open the heavy, copper-studded door and stepped inside. It was warm within, packed with rough oak tables, and permeated with the dense, belly-deep hum of dozens of voices. Behind the counter stood an elderly elven woman with the tired but sharp gaze of a proprietress who had seen life in all its most unattractive manifestations.
'Good evening,' Anar approached the counter, trying to hold himself straight and confident. 'I'd like to rent a room.'
The proprietress swept him with a long, slow, X-ray look. Her nostrils flared slightly.
'You might want to wash first. This is a respectable establishment,' she replied dryly.
'That's exactly why I want to stay here and get cleaned up. Get myself together. I just got out of the dungeon.'
The proprietress's eyebrow slowly climbed upward.
'So you're a criminal too? No, our doors are closed to riffraff like that.'

Anar ground his teeth. His fists clenched at his sides until the knuckles whitened. His head was droning. He was hungry, wrung out, broken, and filthy. Each successive rebuff, each new 'get lost' and 'you're nobody' settled on him stone upon stone, building a wall behind which there was only cold.
'I'm not a criminal,' Anar said hoarsely. 'I promise: I'll wash. There won't be any trouble from me. I just... need one night. Just one.'

He didn't lower his eyes. He didn't grovel. He simply stood and looked at the proprietress with a gaze in which neither pride, nor bravado, nor that trademark Anar laziness remained. Only honest, simple, human exhaustion.
The proprietress stared at him in silence for a long time. Then she sighed — that tired, maternal sigh that is the same in every world — and laid a heavy iron key on the counter.
'Third door down the corridor on the left. Bathhouse is in the basement. Towel's on the bed. If I hear a single noise from you before morning — you'll fly out into the street naked. Understood?'
'Understood. Thank you.'

Anar washed for a long time. Standing under the stream of warm, sulfur-tinted water in the cramped basement bath, he wasn't just scrubbing away the filth of fifteen days. He was scrubbing away the feeling of his own uselessness, which had eaten deeper than any soot. Water flowed down his thin, angular face, over his closed eyes, over the split lip that had already begun to heal. He stood there and silently listened to the drops striking stone.
That night he slept like the dead. For the first time in fifteen days — on something clean, on something dry, in silence.

Morning arrived gray, as every morning in this world did. Anar dressed in clothing the proprietress had laundered — she had silently taken his rags during the night and washed them, saying nothing. Perhaps out of pity. Perhaps out of concern for her establishment's reputation. He didn't ask.
He left the city and went to find his friends.

He found Zhenya first. The Mechanics' Guild was buzzing like an anthill. Brann was furiously hammering a sheet of bluish steel, apprentices darted between workbenches. Zhenya, his face smeared with soot, was fussing over something resembling a small magical turbine. Around him gathered three elf apprentices who followed every movement of his hands with reverential attention.

'Oh, Anar!' Zhenya looked up and smiled sincerely, broadly. 'Bro, where've you been? I figured you were off sleeping somewhere again! Look what we've built — a rotary compressor for defensive turrets on the walls! Third prototype! The first two exploded, but this one — I swear to you — is going to hold!'
Anar looked at Zhenya. At his bright, alive eyes. At his hands, covered in burns and calluses. At how effortlessly, how naturally he had fit into this world — like a gear into a mechanism.
'Nice work, Zhenya,' Anar said quietly. 'Happy for you.'
He moved on.
Yura met him at the mine's surface, tin megaphone in one hand and a stack of inscribed parchments in the other.

'Anar! Listen, I can't talk long — I've got a shift change in ten minutes, and two new ventilation drifts haven't been marked yet. But bro, we've boosted output by thirty percent! Thirty! Can you believe it? The senior overseer said I'm the best foreman in half a century! Me, Yura from our Yekaterinburg!'
Yura was glowing. His nervousness, his chronic anxiety — all of it was still there, but now it worked for him, not against him. He had found his fulcrum.
'Attaboy, Yura,' Anar nodded. 'Proud of you.'
He moved on.

He found Ravan on the training grounds. Ravan, his torso covered in fresh scrapes from sparring, was methodically working with his pneumatic fist. Seeing Anar, Ravan interrupted his set and grinned widely:
'Well, bro, back from vacation? Or are they just airing out your cell? Come on, get in the ring for a spar, I'll knock that lazy nonsense out of you in two seconds!'
Anar just waved him off tiredly. Ravan guffawed, threw a training rag at him, and slapped him encouragingly on the shoulder.
He wasn't angry at them and wasn't offended. They were his brothers, his family, his only reason to stay alive. But his own homelessness and lack of clear purpose weighed on him too heavily.
Potential without purpose is a blank cartridge. Beautiful, shiny, a completely useless piece of metal.

Anar stood at the Eastern Gates of Agdash, staring at the dead gray horizon. Beyond the walls stretched ashen wastelands, eaten by radiation, littered with bones and the wreckage of civilizations. The gate guard — a different one, not the sharp-cheeked one — glanced at him indifferently.
'I want to go out,' said Anar.
The guard raised an eyebrow.
'It's dangerous beyond the walls, outlander. Beasts. Radiation storms. Wandering portals. If you don't have a squad or a combat assignment — I'd advise staying behind the walls.'
'I'm a mage,' Anar said shortly. 'I can handle it.'
The guard shrugged with the fatalism of a creature accustomed to death:
'Your call.'
The heavy gates scraped open just enough to let one person through. Anar stepped outside. The hot, dry wind of the wastelands struck his face, carrying the taste of sulfur and ash.

He walked without direction — just as in the city. But here, beyond the walls, aimlessness felt different. Not like weakness, but like a challenge. As if the world was saying: 'Find it. Find the reason. Or die.'

Anar pushed deeper into the dead forest. Trees stood here like charred skeletons — trunks without bark, branches without leaves, frozen in poses of agony. Gray ash lay on the ground in an even, fluffy carpet, muffling all sound. It was quiet. Frighteningly, ringingly quiet.
After an hour of walking, he heard a sound.
A quiet, rhythmic crackling. A sound like the breaking of thin glass, repeating at regular intervals. Anar moved silently toward it, instinctively pressing against the charred trunks.
In a small clearing surrounded by dead trees, he saw her.

She sat on her knees before a rocky fragment, both palms extended forward. Above her hands, a faint orange glow trembled, pulsed, and then died. Each flash was accompanied by that same glass-like crack — and each time, the glow perished before it could take shape.

She was a fire elf. Young — by elven reckoning, perhaps his age, or slightly younger. Short for an elf, with angular, stubborn features that disrupted the perfect, almost sterile beauty typical of her race. On her head, a reddish flame flickered dimly, fitfully — quite young, uncertain, like a candle flame in the wind. Her clothing was simple — a heavy gray cloak, leather bracers on her wrists, worn boots.
She was trying to cast a spell. And she was failing.

Anar stood watching, his shoulder leaning against a charred trunk. She was fighting so hard over that pitiful spark, trying so agonizingly to shape something from nothing, that Anar's throat suddenly tightened. He saw himself in her — only from the other side of the mirror. He had the power but no purpose. She, evidently, had the purpose — but lacked the power.
The glow died again. The elf girl slammed her fist against the rock and hissed in pain.
'That hurts, you know,' Anar said quietly.
She flinched and spun around sharply. In her eyes — amber-brown, with the characteristic fiery glint — fear flickered, instantly replaced by wariness. One hand instinctively reached for her belt.
'Easy,' Anar raised both palms. A blue spark danced lazily, peacefully between his fingers. 'I'm not some creature from a rift. I'm from Agdash. Just walking.'
'Walking?' She frowned, not lowering her hand. 'Outside the city walls? Alone? You're either brave or insane.'
'The second, probably,' Anar answered without a smile. 'I'm Anar. Human. From another world.'
She paused, studying him. Then, apparently deciding that a thin, exhausted guy with circles under his eyes posed no threat, she slowly straightened.
'Stefani,' she introduced herself curtly. 'I'm from Agdash too.'
'I saw you practicing,' Anar nodded toward the rock. 'Not going well?'
Her face twitched for an instant — that same grimace anyone makes when a stranger casually pokes a sore spot.
'That's none of your business.'
'Fair enough,' Anar conceded easily. He sat down right in the ash, legs crossed, the way he did in a thousand familiar situations — with lazy, disarming ease. 'I'm just curious.'
Stefani sized him up with a skeptical look, but said nothing.
'A human beyond the walls...' she shook her head.
'Listen, Stefani,' Anar shifted to a serious tone. 'I've been wandering around for a while now. Trying to figure out something important. And here's what I thought...'
He went quiet, gazing at the dead horizon. Ashen dunes stretched to the leaden sky.
'Thought what?' Stefani sat down opposite him, involuntarily drawn in.

'Portals. Rifts in the fabric of creation. Everyone keeps saying they can't be closed. That reality is rotting, and there's nothing we can do. But... we did close one. In the mine, underneath Agdash. We just buried it under rocks. Physically blocked it.'
Stefani slowly shook her head.

'That doesn't work. The mine had a small, local rift. You could plug it like a hole in a boat. But the big portals — the creation vortexes — are entirely different. They're alive. They breathe. They expand. If you could just bury them under rocks, we wouldn't be living in conditions like this.'

'But why not use the same approach, just at scale?' Anar leaned forward, and in his eyes, for the first time in weeks, a living, hot spark flickered. 'Find one of the field portals. And instead of a few tons of rock — bring down a mountain on it. Thousands of tons of stone. Using magic.'
'And do you have that kind of magical power?' Stefani raised an eyebrow.
'No,' Anar answered honestly. 'I don't. But you could go to powerful mages. Assemble a group. Tell them: conjure massive boulders, bury the rift. Combined forces.'

Stefani looked at him for a long time. In her eyes appeared something strange — not mockery, not skepticism. Rather — a distant, painful reflection of the same hope that had been born and died so many times in every inhabitant of this world.

'Do you know how many mages we've lost near portals?' she asked quietly. 'The best mages of Agdash? They tried. Not the way you're describing, but... they tried. The vortexes consume mana. Any spell you throw at a portal, it either deflects back or simply devours. It's not a hole in a wall. It's a wound in the very fabric of reality. It doesn't want to be closed.'
Anar said nothing, looking at his hands. The blue spark pulsed quietly between his fingers.
'But rocks aren't mana,' he said slowly. 'Rocks are physics. Mass. Gravity. A portal can devour a spell, but can it devour ten thousand tons of basalt?'

Stefani opened her mouth to reply, but stopped. Her brows drew together. She was thinking — genuinely, deeply, with that inner honesty that distinguishes people searching for truth from those who merely repeat others' words.
'I don't know,' she finally admitted quietly. 'Nobody has tried that. Everyone has fought portals with magic. It never occurred to anyone to... crush them physically. At that scale.'
'Maybe because in this world, everyone's brain is wired for magic,' Anar shrugged. 'And I'm from a world where there is no magic. But there is concrete, rebar, and several billion tons of gravel.'
Above them, the wind moaned. Ash swirled in a fine, prickling ground drift. Stefani shivered, pulling her cloak tighter.

'Even if it's theoretically possible...' she fixed him with a serious, warning look. 'Finding a group of mages willing to approach a vortex — that's suicide. Nobody will go voluntarily. Near a creation portal, nobody survives.'
'If everyone thought that way, Yashil wouldn't have stood with a pipe in front of a thousand beasts,' Anar replied.
He didn't even know where he had learned that name. Maybe he'd heard it from Avotu. Maybe from guards who whispered the legend of the goblin from the Rotting Hollow.
Stefani didn't answer. She stared at the dead trees, at the ashen horizon, at her own hands that couldn't sustain even a simple fire spell.
'I should head back,' she said quietly, rising from the ground. 'It'll be dark soon. Beyond the walls at night, only those born for war survive.'
'I wasn't born for war,' said Anar without moving. 'But I think I was born to ask the questions nobody asks.'
Stefani turned back. Between them hung a silence — heavy, but not hostile. Like the silence of two people standing at the edge of the same abyss.
'Will you be here tomorrow?' Anar asked.
'Maybe,' Stefani answered, and vanished between the black trunks.
Anar remained alone.
He sat in the ash of the dead forest and stared at his hands. Hands that had yet to create anything in this world.
He hadn't found the answer. Hadn't found the purpose.

But somewhere deep down, beneath the layers of laziness, exhaustion, the dungeon, the bruises, and the humiliation, something stirred. Something small. Not a spark — no. More like a shadow of a spark. The outline of a question that hadn't yet become an answer.
Could you bury the void under stone?

Anar rose from the ground. Brushed the ash from his knees. Shoved his hands in his pockets and slowly trudged back toward the walls of Agdash. His silhouette — thin, hunched, solitary — dissolved into the gray, dead light of the fading day.
There was no answer. But for the first time, he wasn't too lazy to look for one.
